ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based sweet snack / glutinous rice confection
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for tangyuan with filling; A0FKP (rice-based sweet snack) used as closest defensible analogous anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tangyuan; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: sesame paste or peanut paste filling

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tangyuan with filling is 3–4 pieces, weighing approximately 120 g, served in a bowl of hot sweet ginger or plain syrup broth.

nutrient_source.title: Tangyuan (with filling) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Tangyuan (with filling), cr_id F07030011. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to tangyuan with filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tangyuan with filling exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glutinous rice (GI ≈ 86, high) is used as the closest defensible analogous food, as glutinous rice flour is the dominant carbohydrate component. The sugar-rich filling further elevates the effective glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (86 × 47.15 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 serving) / 100 = approximately 48.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.25 g/100 g) with low sodium (6 mg/100 g); sugar-driven insulin resistance and weight gain can elevate blood pressure over time
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low at 6 mg/100 g, which is favourable for blood pressure; however, the very high sugar content (23.25 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, both of which raise cardiovascular risk — limit to 1–2 pieces per occasion and avoid sweetened syrup broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high GI glutinous rice flour base (GI ≈ 86) combined with 23.25 g/100 g sugar and 47.15 g/100 g total carbohydrate with only 2.5 g/100 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tangyuan with filling is not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes: the glutinous rice flour wrapper has one of the highest GI values of any staple carbohydrate, sugar exceeds 23 g/100 g, and dietary fibre is insufficient to blunt the glycaemic spike — strict avoidance or very small occasional portions under dietitian supervision are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 5.8 g/100 g from lard or shortening filling, combined with high sugar (23.25 g/100 g) contributing to dyslipidaemia and atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~5.8 g/100 g) from lard-based filling and the high sugar load (23.25 g/100 g) both contribute to raised LDL cholesterol and triglycerides; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small servings and prefer versions made with vegetable oil-based filling.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 23.25 g/100 g and saturated fat ~5.8 g/100 g are both pro-inflammatory; glutinous rice flour drives high glycaemic load promoting inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tangyuan with filling is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the combination of very high sugar, high saturated fat, and a high-GI glutinous rice base, all of which promote systemic inflammation — avoid or strictly limit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tangyuan with filling (汤圆有馅, tāng yuán yǒu xiàn) are traditional Chinese glutinous rice balls made from glutinous rice flour dough encasing a sweet filling, most commonly black sesame paste, peanut paste, or red bean paste mixed with sugar and lard or vegetable shortening. They are boiled and served in hot sweet ginger syrup or plain water broth. The outer skin is soft, chewy, and slightly translucent when cooked, with a dense, rich, sweet filling that melts in the mouth. Tangyuan are a culturally significant food in Chinese and Singaporean Chinese communities, traditionally eaten during the Winter Solstice Festival (Dongzhi) and the Lantern Festival (Yuan Xiao Jie), symbolising family reunion and togetherness. Per 100 g, tangyuan with filling provides 358 kcal, 5.05 g protein, 16.55 g fat (approximately 5.8 g saturated), 47.15 g carbohydrate, 23.25 g sugar, 2.5 g dietary fibre, and only 6 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is very high (approximately 86, analogous to glutinous rice), making this food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The high sugar and saturated fat content also raise concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Despite the low sodium content, the high sugar load poses an indirect hypertension risk through insulin-driven sodium retention. Tangyuan are a seasonal treat best consumed in small portions by the general healthy population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tangyuan with filling?
What is the GI of glutinous rice balls?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F07030011) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented. Saturated fat estimated from typical lard/sesame-paste filling composition consistent with HPB data context.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for tangyuan with filling are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F07030011 and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific traditional preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat (5.8 g/100 g) is estimated from typical lard and sesame-paste filling composition consistent with the HPB data context and is documented in health_context. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for tangyuan with filling exists in any approved source. Halal status is uncertain as traditional recipes use lard; versions using vegetable shortening may be halal — consumers should verify with the specific producer. is_vegan set to false due to lard in traditional filling.
